finding: Amend guard (ii) is stated as "no path other than the task file differing from that commit" but the comparison basis is undefined: working-tree vs HEAD, index vs HEAD, or staged set vs HEAD? A tester cannot construct the guard-(ii)-failure case (acceptance item 4 requires the fallback commit when HEAD moved, but says nothing about how to trigger the path guard), so two engineers would test different things (one touches an unrelated file in the worktree, another stages one).
suggestion: Pin the exact comparison, e.g. "git diff --name-only HEAD (worktree vs HEAD) lists only <base>.tasks.json" and specify whether staged-only or worktree paths count.

finding: The amend-based close checkpoint creates a commit-sha self-reference paradox the spec never resolves. Flow as stated: tp creates a commit (task still wip) → writes the closure record (status=done, commit_sha=???) → folds it in via `git commit --amend`. But the commit_sha written inside the task file must equal the sha of the commit that *contains* that task file. After amend the containing sha is unknowable in advance (amend produces a new sha), and re-amending to correct it loops forever (C1→amend→C2 with sha C1; fix to C2→amend→C3 with sha C2; …). The spec simultaneously demands (a) `git status` is clean when the command returns, (b) 'the recorded sha is the sha the task file carries', and (c) the commit contains the post-close state. No tuple satisfies all three under a single amend. Today's code sidesteps this by committing the pre-close file and writing commit_sha afterwards (leaving the tree dirty) — the very defect §5.1 sets out to fix — so the spec is asking the implementer to solve an impossible constraint without naming the compromise.
suggestion: Pin the resolution explicitly. Either (a) commit_sha records the pre-amend sha and the spec states the on-disk file matches HEAD post-amend (accept the sha points at the closure commit, defined as 'the commit whose tree contains the done state'), (b) commit_sha is left null in the amend and written only to the in-memory/output result (accept the committed file carries null), or (c) abandon amend and use a single commit that stages the already-closed task file with commit_sha populated by a two-phase commit (stage closed state with placeholder, commit, read sha, restage with real sha, commit --amend once — accepting one level of sha drift). Name which.

finding: "tp resume's next_action for that state points at completing and recording round N" does not pin the literal value/shape of next_action. Acceptance item 12 asserts the behavior but a tester cannot write the equality assertion without the exact string or object next_action carries for an in-flight round.
suggestion: Specify the next_action value, e.g. {"action":"record-round","round":N} or a fixed string template, consistent with the existing next_action vocabulary.

finding: "Every error object tp emits for a rejected invocation carries a hint" leaves "rejected invocation" undefined. It is unclear whether this covers only exit-2 usage errors, or also exit-1 validation, exit-3 file, and exit-4 state errors (e.g. the lock-timeout error in §12.2). A tester cannot decide whether an exit-4 runtime error missing a hint is a defect.
suggestion: Define "rejected invocation" by exit code (e.g. "every non-zero exit except where the spec states a bare message"), or enumerate the exit codes the hint requirement applies to.

finding: "When actual_minutes rounds to 0.0, accuracy is null" — the rounding precision (decimal places) is not specified, so the null-boundary is unpinned. A task taking 0.04 min could round to 0.0 at 1 dp but to 0.04 at 2 dp; two testers pick different cutoffs. Acceptance item 16's "under a second" is an example, not the rule.
suggestion: Pin the rounding (e.g. "actual_minutes is rounded to 1 decimal place; accuracy is null when that value is 0.0") or define the boundary in raw duration (e.g. "< 6 seconds").

finding: §16.2 lists `auto` as a valid `commit_strategy` value (`<builtin|auto|hc>`) and makes it settable at the project level, but §5 (the close checkpoint) only specifies behavior for `builtin` (§5.1: tp commits, amend guards) and `hc` (§5.2: tp classifies, bookkeeping array). An implementer building the close checkpoint cannot decide what `tp done --auto-commit` does when `commit_strategy: auto` — does it follow the builtin amend path (§5.1) or the hc classify path (§5.2)? The exit-code/contract test §19.4 ('After tp done --auto-commit, git status is clean…') presupposes builtin semantics, so if `auto` maps to hc the test is wrong, and if it maps to builtin the spec should say so.
suggestion: Add one sentence to §5 stating how `auto` resolves at close time (e.g. 'auto behaves as builtin when a git repo is present and hc otherwise', or 'auto is an alias for builtin for the close checkpoint'). Alternatively drop `auto` from §16.2's accepted values if it is not intended to be settable.

finding: The success contract mixes "empty stdout in NDJSON mode" with "a summary whose merged_count is 0" without saying where the summary goes (stdout? stderr? the -o file?) or what "NDJSON mode" means for `--merge` (there is no documented --ndjson flag for merge; is it --json, or piping?). A tester cannot assert both "stdout empty" and "summary present" without knowing they live on different streams.
suggestion: State explicitly: findings stream target (-o file), summary target (stdout JSON when --json/piped, else stderr), and define or drop the "NDJSON mode" phrase.

finding: "a spec-looking positional is never consumed as data" — the criterion for "spec-looking" is undefined. Is it any non-flag positional? A path ending in .md? A positional that appears before the data files? Acceptance item 3 tests two concrete invocations but the general rule is not testable: a tester cannot predict whether `tp audit --merge a.md b.ndjson` or `tp review --resolve findings.ndjson spec.md 0 ...` is rejected.
suggestion: Define the rule precisely, e.g. "any positional argument present in --merge/--resolve/--resolve-all modes" or "any positional whose value does not resolve to a readable .ndjson file", and list it for each affected mode.

finding: Guard (ii) — 'no path other than the task file differing from that commit' — does not account for other tp-owned writes that may occur during a close. `tp done` runs the quality gate and may write `.tp-review/` state (e.g. a gate-result record), and under `tp commit --files` the caller can stage arbitrary paths. If any tp-owned path other than the task file is modified during the close, guard (ii) fails and every close falls back to the follow-up commit — silently defeating the checkpoint feature for the exact cases the spec is trying to fix. The spec must define whether 'the task file' means literally one path or 'tp-owned closure state' (task file + any .tp-review closure record).
suggestion: State explicitly whether the amend guard compares only the single task-file path or the set of tp-owned paths written during the close, and if the latter, enumerate which paths (task file, .tp-review gate record, etc.) are folded into the amend.

finding: `git commit --amend` produces a NEW commit sha, so a closing commit that folds the closure record into itself via amend cannot self-reference its own containing sha. The spec says 'the recorded sha is the sha the task file carries', but the task file is committed *inside* the amended commit — the sha is not knowable at content-write time, and after amend the object sha differs from any sha written into the file. §5.1 never states whether commit_sha/commit_shas holds the pre-amend sha (the tp-commit C1) or the post-amend sha (C2). This is load-bearing for §11.1, whose `suggested_files` is 'the union of paths touched by the commits recorded in commit_shas' — the wrong choice silently breaks audit file derivation.
suggestion: State explicitly which sha commit_sha/commit_shas carries after an amend (the natural choice is the pre-amend implementation commit C1, since that is the commit whose tree holds the audited source files), and acknowledge that the post-amend object sha differs. Alternatively, drop amend and always emit the follow-up closure commit so the recorded sha is exact.

finding: §5.1 is a single sub-section but bundles at least five independently testable concerns: (1) reorder write-then-commit to commit-closed-state, (2) the `git commit --amend --no-edit` fold, (3) the two HEAD/path guards, (4) the follow-up-commit fallback with its `chore(tp): record <id> closure` message, and (5) the clean-tree guarantee plus sha-recording contract. Each has its own failure modes and tests. Treating this as one task guarantees either a sprawling commit or an under-tested one; the spec gives no decomposition guidance and §19.4 only tests the happy + HEAD-moved paths, leaving the guard-(ii)-failure path untested at acceptance level.
suggestion: Split §5.1 into named sub-items (5.1a commit-reorders-closure, 5.1b amend-fold, 5.1c guards, 5.1d fallback-commit) so each can be a single atomic commit with its own acceptance row, or explicitly state that §5.1 is one atomic unit and add acceptance rows for the guard-(ii)-failure and sha-recording paths.

finding: The `bookkeeping` array is introduced but each entry's shape is undefined. "each entry carrying the closure or round it records" does not name the fields (path? closure id? round number? kind?). Acceptance item 5 can only assert that the task file appears somewhere under bookkeeping, not its structure, so the contract two engineers test against diverges.
suggestion: Specify the entry object, e.g. {"path":"<base>.tasks.json","kind":"closure","ref":"<task-id>"} (and "round"/N for review state), and state whether the array is [] or omitted when empty.

finding: `tp resume`'s new `bookkeeping` array requires each entry to carry 'the closure or round it records' — but the spec never says how tp infers that mapping. A modified `<base>.tasks.json` could reflect any combination of closures, reopens, sets, and adds since the last commit; a dirty `.tp-review/round-N.ndjson` could be a recorded round whose commit was lost or an in-flight round. tp would have to diff the file against HEAD and parse the semantic delta (which tasks changed status, which round file was written) to label each entry. That is a non-trivial inference problem and the spec treats it as if the label is simply available. Without the inference rule pinned, two implementers produce different `bookkeeping` payloads.
suggestion: Specify the attribution source: e.g. 'for the task file, tp diffs against HEAD and attributes one bookkeeping entry per task whose managed fields (status, closed_at, gate_passed_at, commit_sha) changed, labeled with that task id; for .tp-review files, the entry is labeled by the round number parsed from the filename'.

finding: The `in_flight_round` invariant — 'a snapshot with no round file means a round was started and never recorded' — is only true if `snapshot-round-N.md` is written at round START and `review-round-N.ndjson` at record time. §10.3 frames the snapshot as the *baseline for the NEXT round's regression role*, which reads as 'written when the round is recorded'. The spec never states the write ordering. If snapshots are written at record time (alongside the round file), the 'snapshot without round file' state can never arise and §10.2's detection is dead; if at start time, §10.3's baseline framing needs that stated. Either way the write sequence is an unstated dependency.
suggestion: Add one sentence to §10.2 pinning the sequence: 'tp writes snapshot-round-N.md when round N begins (prompt emission) and review-round-N.ndjson when round N is recorded (--record)', making the in-flight invariant well-founded and reconciling it with §10.3's baseline use.

finding: `in_flight_round: N` is derived from 'a snapshot with no round file', but the spec does not require the snapshot write to be atomic. If tp writes `snapshot-round-N.md` non-atomically (open, truncate, write, close) and the process is killed mid-write, a partial/empty snapshot file is left on disk and is indistinguishable from a completed snapshot whose round was never recorded. `tp resume` would then point an agent at 'completing round N' when the snapshot itself is corrupt. The detection rule (snapshot present ∧ round file absent ⇒ in-flight) is only sound if snapshot writes are crash-safe.
suggestion: Require atomic snapshot writes (write to `snapshot-round-N.md.tmp` then rename) and state that a `.tmp` file is ignored by the in-flight check, or define the in-flight signal as a separate sentinel file created before the snapshot write and removed after the round is recorded.

finding: §10.3 says the regression prompt names `snapshot-round-<N-1>.md` as its baseline, but for the first review round (N=1) this resolves to `snapshot-round-0.md`, which is never created (rounds start at 1, and a snapshot is written per started round). The spec does not say whether the regression role is skipped for round 1, emits an empty-diff prompt, or errors. Given §9.1 now requires every non-emitted role to appear in `skipped_roles` with a reason, the round-1 regression handling must be defined or it will surface as an unattributed omission — exactly what §2.4 ('silence is a bug') forbids.
suggestion: Add: 'for round 1, the regression role is emitted with an empty baseline (diff against the empty tree) and a note in the prompt, OR is added to skipped_roles with reason no-baseline' — pick one and state it.

finding: "the union of paths touched by the commits recorded in commit_shas of done tasks" — the scope of "done tasks" is unpinned: all done tasks in the file, or only those done since some baseline? For a task that was done, reopened, and done again (commit_shas may hold multiple SHAs), which SHAs contribute? A tester cannot construct the expected suggested_files set deterministically.
suggestion: Pin the scope: "all tasks with status=done in the active task file, using every SHA in their commit_shas" (and state whether reopened-then-redone tasks contribute all historical SHAs or only the latest).

finding: `suggested_files` is 'the union of paths touched by the commits recorded in commit_shas of done tasks'. A task closed via `--covered-by` (§4 of the command reference) has empty commit_shas, and a task closed before commit_shas existed (legacy) likewise. When every done task has empty commit_shas, suggested_files is empty. The spec does not say whether `tp audit` then still exits 4 (no audit-able file) with an empty suggested_files array, or exits 0, or falls back to a git-diff derivation. The acceptance row §19.13 only tests the happy path ('a fully committed tree').
suggestion: Define the empty-commit_shas case: 'when suggested_files is empty, tp audit still exits 4 with suggested_files: [] and a hint naming --affected-files / --affected-from-tasks' — or state the fallback derivation.

finding: "a hint naming the lock path and the elapsed wait" — the unit/format of "elapsed wait" is unspecified (seconds? milliseconds? integer? rounded?). The hint text contract is not deterministic enough to assert exactly.

resolved.evidence: elapsed-wait unit is hint-text detail; implementer pins seconds

suggestion: Pin the unit, e.g. "the hint includes the elapsed wait in seconds, rounded to 1 decimal place, suffixed with 's'".

finding: "Any cobra flag-parse failure" is an open-ended class with no enumeration or defining criterion. The two example rows are testable, but the generalized "any" cannot be tested exhaustively; a tester cannot decide whether a new cobra failure mode (e.g. unknown subcommand, unknown long flag) is in scope.

resolved.evidence: cobra 'any' bounded by the two concrete §13.1 rows; enumeration deferred to impl tests

suggestion: Either drop the "any" and enumerate the specific cases, or define the class by the cobra error type tp wraps (e.g. "errors of kind pflag.ErrHelp / unknown-flag / invalid-argument"), and state that all such errors return a tp error object at exit 2.

finding: "the summary reports how many were excluded" does not name the field. A tester can assert the exclusion behavior but not the JSON key, so the summary-shape contract is left to interpretation.

resolved.evidence: summary already reports excluded count; exact JSON key is impl detail

suggestion: Name the field, e.g. "excluded_from_accuracy":<n>, alongside estimation_accuracy.

finding: §3.1 says 'an empty output file at -o (created, zero bytes), empty stdout in NDJSON mode' for the all-empty case, but does not state whether `-o` is required or optional for `--merge`. If `-o` is omitted, does tp write findings to stdout (and thus write nothing / a newline for the empty case)? If `-o` is required, the exit-2 'no input files' path must still validate `-o` presence before failing. The current `--merge` implementation's `-o` contract is not referenced, so an implementer does not know whether to enforce `-o` before or after the input-count check, which affects which exit code wins when both are wrong.
suggestion: State whether `-o` is required for `--merge`, and if so, the order of validation (inputs checked before or after `-o` presence) so the exit-code precedence is deterministic.

finding: "Malformed or incomplete lines" are both skipped, but the distinction between "malformed" and "incomplete" is undefined. A line that is valid JSON but missing a required field — is that malformed or incomplete, and does the distinction matter for the warning text or exit code? A tester cannot classify a line to predict the warning.
suggestion: Define both terms (e.g. "malformed = not valid JSON; incomplete = valid JSON missing a required finding field") and state whether the stderr warning text differs, or collapse to a single term.

finding: The §3.3 matrix has a row 'An input file missing or unreadable → exit 3' and a row 'No input files given → exit 2', but it does not cover the case where input file paths ARE given on the command line yet every one of them is missing on disk. Is that 'an input file missing' (exit 3, applied to the first) or 'no input files' (exit 2, since zero were readable)? The two rows give different exit codes for what a caller experiences as the same outcome ('I passed paths, none existed'). An implementer must choose, and §19.1 only tests 'a missing input file exits 3' (singular) and 'no input files exits 2' (zero given), not the all-missing intersection.
suggestion: Add a matrix row or a clarifying sentence: 'if at least one input path was given but none could be opened, exit 3 (the caller intended files to exist); exit 2 is reserved for zero input paths given on the command line'.

finding: §4.1 says 'a spec-looking positional is never consumed as data' in --merge/--resolve modes, but never defines 'spec-looking'. Is it any positional (since --merge/--resolve take only flag arguments and file paths)? Is it a path ending in `.md`? A path containing a heading? Today's bug is that `tp audit <spec> --merge a.ndjson b.ndjson` parses the spec as input file #1 — so the fix must decide: does tp reject ANY positional that precedes the mode flags, or only positionals matching a heuristic? If the former, the error message 'naming the correct form' must distinguish 'you passed a spec positional to --merge' from 'you passed an unknown positional'. The detection rule is the contract.
suggestion: Define the rule precisely: 'in --merge and --resolve modes, tp accepts exactly zero positionals; any positional argument is rejected with exit 2 and a hint showing the mode's expected argument shape' — replacing the vague 'spec-looking' with a concrete zero-positional rule.

finding: §2.2 promises that after a close through tp's own commit path, tp-owned state is committed and the working tree is clean. But §5.1's amend (and its fallback follow-up commit) fold ONLY the task file — guard (ii) explicitly fails if 'no path other than the task file differs'. If any other tp-owned path is dirty at close time (.tp-review/ from a just-recorded round, .tp/config.json, a migrated .tp/.gitignore), guard (ii) trips, the fallback fires, and that fallback is described as closure-only ('chore(tp): record <id> closure') — leaving the other tp-owned state uncommitted and the tree NOT clean, violating §2.2. The spec never states the precondition that all non-task-file tp state is already committed before the close, nor that the amend/fallback stages those paths.
suggestion: Define the checkpoint's stage set explicitly: either (a) state the precondition that tp commit / the prior round-record already committed all tp-owned paths except the task file, so guard (ii) is met by construction, or (b) have the amend and the fallback follow-up also stage all tp-owned paths (.tp-review/, .tp/) so §2.2's clean-tree guarantee holds regardless of what else is dirty.

finding: The spec adds new output fields across seven features (bookkeeping in resume, skipped_roles in prompt emission, attribution_excludes in merge/status, overlap_report in audit merge/status, max_rounds and rounds_remaining in status, in_flight_round in status, note in report entries) but specifies --compact behavior for none of them. §8.3 is the sole new field with an explicit compact policy ('--compact continues to omit spec_excerpt entirely'), establishing that each new field needs its own compact decision — yet every other new field skips this. --compact is the token-minimizing mode tp exists to serve (P3: minimal tokens). Without per-field compact policy, an agent on --compact cannot predict which new fields survive, and the implementer has no spec guidance: decision-critical fields (max_rounds, in_flight_round, bookkeeping) risk being stripped, while explanatory ones (attribution_excludes, skipped_roles when empty, overlap_report) risk always present — eroding the ~40% reduction --compact promises as more fields accumulate across versions.
suggestion: For each new field, state whether --compact keeps or strips it, mirroring the §8.3 pattern. Keep decision-critical fields under compact (max_rounds, rounds_remaining, in_flight_round, bookkeeping path+task_id); strip explanatory fields (attribution_excludes, skipped_roles when empty, overlap_report, note). One sentence per field, consistent with §8.3's phrasing.

finding: The lock relocates to `.tp/locks/<base>.lock`, but the spec does not say what happens when `.tp/` does not exist (e.g. a task file operated on outside any tp-initialised project, or before `tp init` has run). §5.4 says `tp init` creates `.tp/.gitignore`, implying `.tp/` is created at init — but `tp add`/`tp set`/`tp done` can target a `--file` pointing at an arbitrary path with no `.tp/` ancestor. Does tp create `.tp/locks/` lazily, fall back to the sibling lock, or error? An implementer must pick one, and the stale-sibling-removal logic in the same section presumes the new path is usable.

resolved.evidence: .tp/ created by tp init (§5.4); --file outside tp project is edge case, impl handles fallback

suggestion: Specify: 'tp creates `.tp/locks/` on first write if absent; if `.tp/` cannot be created (e.g. `--file` outside any writable parent), tp falls back to the sibling `<base>.tasks.json.lock` and notes the fallback in stderr' — or declare `.tp/` a hard prerequisite and exit 3 when absent.

finding: The `id` rule rejects values that are "missing, blank, or whitespace-only", but the `title` and `acceptance` rules reject only "missing or blank". "Blank" is not defined; if it does not include whitespace-only, then a title of "   " passes — contradicting the intent the id row makes explicit. Two testers read "blank" differently.
suggestion: Define "blank" once (e.g. "blank = empty after trimming whitespace") and apply the identical wording to id, title, and acceptance rows.

finding: The entry-validation table says `depends_on referencing an unknown task → exit 1`, but tp add has `--bulk` (NDJSON) and `--stdin` paths. In a bulk batch, task A may legitimately depend_on task B that appears later in the same batch. If validation runs per-line at parse time, A is rejected because B is not yet in the file; if it runs after the whole batch is staged, the rule's wording ('already present in the file') is ambiguous about whether pending batch entries count. The spec only says 'tp add applies the same rules tp import applies' — but tp import's handling of intra-batch forward references is also undocumented here, so the implementer has no normative reference.
suggestion: State whether intra-batch references are resolved (validate after staging the whole batch, so forward refs are allowed) or rejected (per-line validation). Reference the exact import behavior or specify it inline.

finding: §8.3 is the only place that pins --compact behavior for a new output field (it omits spec_excerpt). The other new output surfaces — `bookkeeping` (§5.2), `skipped_roles` + `attribution_excludes` (§9.1/§9.2), audit `overlap_report` (§9.3), and `max_rounds`/`rounds_remaining`/`in_flight_round` (§10.1/§10.2) — add bytes to every emit yet say nothing about --compact, tp's primary token-reduction flag (P3). An implementer has no contract for whether these survive --compact, and the omission is inconsistent with §8.3's explicit treatment of spec_excerpt.
suggestion: For each new field, state its --compact disposition (omitted, or always-present because it removes a round-trip). At minimum, declare that skipped_roles/overlap_report are omitted under --compact while the scalar budget fields (max_rounds/rounds_remaining/in_flight_round) and attribution_excludes remain, so P3 holds.

finding: "skipped_roles: an array of {role, reason}" does not specify the empty case. When every corpus role is emitted (zero skips), is skipped_roles [] or omitted? Given §6.2's normalization theme, a tester needs the explicit rule to avoid a null-vs-[] divergence.
suggestion: State "skipped_roles is [] when no roles were skipped" (consistent with §6.2/§6.3 normalization).

finding: §14.1 says 'When actual_minutes rounds to 0.0, accuracy is null' but does not define the rounding precision. '0.0' suggests one decimal place, but the existing report may use two (e.g. 0.04 minutes → 0.0 at one decimal, 0.04 at two). The threshold for the null/notes special case changes with the precision: at one decimal, any task under 0.05 min (3s) triggers it; at two decimals, only sub-0.005-min tasks do. Without the precision pinned, the acceptance test §19.16 ('closed in under a second') is ambiguous about whether 0.5s (0.0083 min) should hit the special case at two-decimal precision.
suggestion: State the rounding precision explicitly (e.g. 'actual_minutes is rounded to one decimal place; values rounding to 0.0 trigger the special case') and ensure §19.16's 'under a second' aligns with that precision.

finding: "tp set --workflow commit_strategy=… explains this clearly" is vague (required message text unpinned), and "answers 'unknown workflow field' with a different exit code for the same concept" names a "different exit code" without stating what it is. The latter is largely superseded by §16.2 (project path becomes a success), but as written the requirement is not testable for message content or the historic code.

resolved.evidence: §16.2 pins the project setter; historic exit-code motivation superseded

suggestion: Replace "explains this clearly" with the required hint text (§16.2 already pins "names the project-level setter"); either drop the "different exit code" motivation or state the pre-release code it refers to.

finding: §16.1 asserts commit_strategy 'cannot be changed afterwards through any documented command'; §16.2 then says 'The project default becomes settable'. A reader hits an apparent contradiction before reaching the sentence that scopes the immutability to the task-file layer ('The task-file value stays init-authored'). The two-layer distinction (project default mutable, task-file value frozen) is correct but the §16.1 absolute phrasing undercuts it.

resolved.evidence: §16.1/§16.2 two-layer distinction stated; phrasing softening is cosmetic

suggestion: Soften §16.1 to 'cannot be changed at the task-file layer through any documented command; the project default is settable per §16.2' so the layer split is stated up front rather than recovered from §16.2.

finding: Acceptance item 8 requires a spec "whose sections are all anchored" but "anchored" is not defined. It could mean every heading has a task, or every task-bearing section is anchored. A tester cannot construct the passing fixture without the criterion.

resolved.evidence: acceptance fixtures defined per-case in §19; 'anchored' reads clearly in context

suggestion: Define "anchored" — e.g. "every task's source_sections resolves to a heading, and the spec has no unanchored leaf heading required for coverage" — or rephrase the acceptance to state the concrete fixture property.

finding: "id already present in the file" does not state whether id matching is case-sensitive. A tester cannot decide whether "Task-1" and "task-1" collide. Most likely exact/string-equal, but it is not pinned.
suggestion: State "id comparison is exact, case-sensitive string equality".

finding: §6.1 defines the add-validation table 'tp add applies the same rules tp import applies, at entry' — i.e. by reference to import's behavior — then lists 7 rules. The spec never confirms that import *currently* enforces all 7 (notably 'depends_on referencing an unknown task → reject'). If import is lenient on any one (e.g. allows forward references during bulk import), then add (strict) and import (lenient) diverge, directly contradicting the stated parity, and §6.2's 'whichever command wrote it' normalization claim loses its foundation.
suggestion: Either state explicitly that import already enforces exactly these 7 rules (so the table is a restatement, no import change), or list the rules independently of import and mark any rule import does NOT yet enforce as a change to import in this release.

finding: §7.1 says AutoFillCoverage runs after 'any command that changes the task set or a task's anchors — add, import, remove, and set of source_sections/source_lines'. Status-changing commands (claim, close, done, reopen) also write the task file but do not change anchors or the task set's coverage-relevant shape. The enumeration reads as exhaustive ('add, import, remove, and set of…'), implying claim/close/done do NOT trigger coverage — but a literal reading of 'any command that changes the task set' would include them (the set of done tasks changes). An implementer does not know whether to call AutoFillCoverage in the claim/close/done write path, affecting performance (coverage re-reads the spec) and correctness (does a done task's coverage get refreshed).
suggestion: Clarify that coverage is recomputed only when anchor-bearing fields change, and that status-only writes (claim/close/done/reopen) skip the recompute — or list the exact commands that trigger it.

finding: attribution_excludes is always ["regression"] — the value never varies because tp's regression-exclusion policy is fixed and not caller-configurable. §19 item 10 confirms it is emitted on BOTH merge summary and --status output, unconditionally. The field exists so an agent seeing a roleless finding in the overlap report reads the absence as policy rather than data loss, but the explanation is byte-identical on every call: after the first encounter the agent has learned it and every subsequent merge/status call pays the same context tax for a constant it cannot act on. This does not satisfy §2.6 ('No new knobs unless a knob removes a round-trip') — the field is explanatory metadata, not a decision input. The investigation it prevents ('where is the missing finding?') only arises when merged_count exceeds the overlap-report finding count, yet the field is emitted even when no regression-only finding exists and the counts already match.
suggestion: Emit attribution_excludes only when a regression-only finding actually causes merged_count to exceed the overlap-report finding count — the single scenario where the absence would otherwise trigger investigation. When counts match, omit the field entirely (or strip it under --compact per the compact-policy finding). Alternatively, document the exclusion once in REFERENCE.md (§17.2 already lists it there) and drop the runtime field.
